refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall establish and maintain end-to-end teleoperation communication with p95 latency ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions to enable safe remote operation by tele-operators.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-008 addresses a critical safety and operational requirement. Teleoperation latency directly impacts operator situational awareness, control responsiveness, and safety margins. The p95 percentile metric ensures consistent performance under variable network conditions rather than optimistic average-case assumptions. High safety criticality demands explicit latency budgeting and verification.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure end-to-end latency (command transmission → vehicle sensor acquisition → telemetry reception at operator console) across representative network conditions (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss) using instrumented test scenarios. Collect p95 statistics over minimum 1000 samples per condition.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The teleoperation system shall acquire and encode vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor data) at ≥ 10 Hz minimum.
FR-NGHE-008.2: The teleoperation system shall transmit operator commands (movement, tool control) with deterministic latency budgeting.
FR-NGHE-008.3: The teleoperation system shall receive and decode remote commands with bounded processing delay.
FR-NGHE-008.4: The network link shall support bidirectional communication with latency contribution ≤ 100 ms p95 under agreed conditions.
FR-NGHE-008.5: The teleoperation system shall detect and alert on latency degradation or communication loss.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement low-latency teleoperation stack with hardware-accelerated encoding/decoding, prioritized network queuing, and predictive command buffering to minimize end-to-end latency.
DP-B: Deploy edge computing node co-located with vehicle to reduce round-trip latency by offloading sensor fusion and command preprocessing.
DP-C: Establish dedicated network link (private LTE, licensed spectrum) with QoS guarantees and latency SLA monitoring to bound network contribution.
DP-D: Implement hybrid teleoperation with local autonomy fallback to reduce operator command frequency and tolerate higher latency.
DP-E: Combine low-latency stack (DP-A) + dedicated network link (DP-C) + latency monitoring to achieve p95 ≤ 150 ms through multi-layer optimization.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-E: Combine low-latency teleoperation stack (hardware-accelerated encoding, prioritized queuing, minimal processing hops) with dedicated network link (QoS-enabled, latency SLA monitoring) and real-time latency instrumentation to achieve p95 ≤ 150 ms end-to-end latency under agreed network conditions.

potential_sources: DP-E couples teleoperation software stack (encoding/decoding, command processing) with network infrastructure (QoS, link provisioning). This is acceptable because latency is fundamentally a system-level property requiring coordinated optimization across both layers.
DP-E couples vehicle sensor acquisition timing with telemetry transmission scheduling. This is necessary and manageable through explicit timing contracts.
DP-E does not create circular dependencies or conflicting design constraints.
coupling_check.notes: The coupling is intentional and justified by the safety-critical latency requirement. Decoupling would require relaxing the p95 ≤ 150 ms constraint or accepting higher risk.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Agreed network conditions are defined and stable (bandwidth ≥ X Mbps, jitter ≤ Y ms, packet loss ≤ Z%).
ASSUMPTION: Dedicated network link provisioning is feasible within project budget and schedule.
ASSUMPTION: Vehicle hardware supports ≥ 10 Hz sensor acquisition and real-time encoding.
RISK: If network conditions degrade beyond agreed parameters, p95 ≤ 150 ms may not be achievable without fallback to local autonomy.
RISK: Hardware-accelerated encoding/decoding adds cost and complexity; failure modes must be analyzed.
RISK: Latency instrumentation overhead could introduce measurement artifacts; calibration required.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Explicitly define network condition SLAs (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss ranges) and establish fallback strategy (local autonomy, operator alert, mission abort) if conditions degrade. Allocate latency budget across sub-components: vehicle acquisition (20 ms), encoding (10 ms), network (100 ms), decoding (10 ms), operator display (10 ms) = 150 ms total.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Owns end-to-end teleoperation latency performance. Encompasses command reception, encoding/decoding, scheduling, and latency monitoring. Interfaces with Vehicle (for sensor data and command actuation), Network Link (for transmission), and Safety Services (for latency degradation alerts).

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module transmits vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor readings, system status) to operator console. Latency-critical; must include timestamp for latency measurement.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module receives operator commands (movement, tool control, mode changes) from remote operator console. Must support deterministic processing and command queuing.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module uses Network Link for bidirectional communication. Depends on Network Link to meet latency SLA (≤ 100 ms p95). Provides latency telemetry to Network Link for monitoring.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module sends alerts to Safety Services when latency exceeds threshold or communication is lost. Enables safety-critical response (operator notification, mission abort).

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of latency optimization logic, encoding/decoding, command processing, and latency instrumentation. All sub-functions align with teleoperation mission. Minimal diffusion: only external dependency is Network Link for transmission.

Vehicle owns sensor acquisition and command actuation, but does not own teleoperation protocol, encoding, or latency optimization. Assigning DP-E to Vehicle would scatter teleoperation logic across vehicle and operator console, creating diffusion and ownership ambiguity.

Network Link owns network-layer latency (transmission, routing, QoS). However, DP-E includes application-layer optimization (encoding, scheduling, buffering) which is outside Network Link scope. Partial containment only.

Safety Services owns safety response logic, not teleoperation performance optimization. Assigning DP-E to Safety Services would be a category error. Safety Services consumes SafetyAlert from Teleoperation.
NGHE Solution is the system-level container but does not own specific teleoperation implementation. Assigning DP-E to NGHE Solution would leave implementation responsibility undefined.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation module is the correct owner of DP-E because it encompasses the full teleoperation stack (encoding, decoding, command processing, latency instrumentation) and has clear ownership of latency performance. External dependencies (Vehicle sensor data, Network Link transmission, Safety Services alerts) are managed via well-defined interfaces.
containment_risks: RISK: If Vehicle hardware cannot support ≥ 10 Hz sensor acquisition, Teleoperation cannot achieve latency target. Mitigation: Establish hardware requirements in Vehicle specification.

RISK: If Network Link cannot guarantee ≤ 100 ms p95 latency, Teleoperation cannot achieve system-level target. Mitigation: Establish network SLA in Network Link specification and define fallback strategy.
RISK: Latency instrumentation overhead could introduce measurement artifacts. Mitigation: Calibrate instrumentation and validate against independent measurement tools.
RISK: Encoding/decoding hardware acceleration adds cost and introduces new failure modes. Mitigation: Conduct FMEA on hardware acceleration components and establish redundancy strategy.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Establish explicit latency budget allocation across Teleoperation sub-components and define interface contracts with Vehicle (sensor data format, acquisition rate) and Network Link (transmission latency SLA). Create detailed latency instrumentation specification to enable verification.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-E (combined low-latency stack + dedicated network link + latency monitoring) directly addresses FR-NGHE-008 (p95 ≤ 150 ms teleoperation latency). The DP is specific, measurable, and includes verification mechanisms (latency instrumentation). No logical gaps or contradictions.

diffusion_risks: Teleoperation module depends on Vehicle for sensor data and Network Link for transmission. These dependencies are external but necessary and manageable via interface contracts.

Latency instrumentation requires coordination with Vehicle (timestamp sensor data) and Network Link (report transmission latency). Mitigation: Define instrumentation protocol in interface specifications.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation achieves 95% containment score. The 5% diffusion (external dependencies) is unavoidable and well-managed. No alternative module provides better containment without creating larger diffusion problems.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible via instrumented testing under agreed network conditions. Measurement approach is clear: capture end-to-end latency (command transmission → vehicle response) and compute p95 percentile. Acceptance criteria (p95 ≤ 150 ms) is objective and testable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation owns latency optimization and instrumentation. Vehicle owns sensor acquisition timing. Network Link owns transmission latency. Safety Services owns response to latency degradation. Interface contracts define responsibilities at boundaries.

recommended_revisions: Establish detailed latency budget allocation: Vehicle acquisition (20 ms), Teleoperation encoding (10 ms), Network transmission (100 ms), Teleoperation decoding (10 ms), operator display (10 ms). Document in design specification.
Define network condition SLAs (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss) and fallback strategy if conditions degrade.
Create latency instrumentation specification with timestamp formats, measurement points, and calibration procedures.
Establish interface contracts between Teleoperation, Vehicle, Network Link, and Safety Services with explicit latency requirements and data formats.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-008 is not atomic; it decomposes into five sub-FRs (sensor acquisition, command transmission, command reception, network link, latency monitoring). DP-E is sound and well-allocated to Teleoperation module. However, the sub-FRs require further decomposition to establish detailed design and verification strategy. Proceeding to next level is necessary to allocate latency budget, define interface contracts, and establish instrumentation approach.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The teleoperation system shall acquire and encode vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor data) at ≥ 10 Hz minimum.
FR-NGHE-008.2: The teleoperation system shall transmit operator commands (movement, tool control) with deterministic latency budgeting.
FR-NGHE-008.3: The teleoperation system shall receive and decode remote commands with bounded processing delay.
FR-NGHE-008.4: The network link shall support bidirectional communication with latency contribution ≤ 100 ms p95 under agreed conditions.
FR-NGHE-008.5: The teleoperation system shall detect and alert on latency degradation or communication loss.

assumptions: Agreed network conditions are defined and stable (bandwidth ≥ X Mbps, jitter ≤ Y ms, packet loss ≤ Z%).
Vehicle hardware supports ≥ 10 Hz sensor acquisition and real-time encoding.
Dedicated network link provisioning is feasible within project budget and schedule.
Operator console hardware can support real-time decoding and display at ≥ 10 Hz.
Latency instrumentation can be implemented without introducing measurement artifacts.
open_risks: Network conditions may degrade beyond agreed parameters, making p95 ≤ 150 ms unachievable.
Hardware-accelerated encoding/decoding adds cost and introduces new failure modes.
Latency instrumentation overhead could introduce measurement artifacts.
Vehicle sensor acquisition timing may not be deterministic, affecting latency predictability.
Operator console display latency (not controlled by NGHE) could exceed allocated budget.
evidence_needed: Network condition SLA specification (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss ranges).
Vehicle hardware specification (sensor acquisition rate, processing capability).
Operator console specification (display latency, processing capability).
Detailed latency budget allocation across sub-components.
Latency instrumentation specification with timestamp formats and measurement points.
Test plan for verification under agreed network conditions (minimum 1000 samples per condition).
FMEA on hardware acceleration components and redundancy strategy.
Fallback strategy if network conditions degrade (local autonomy, operator alert, mission abort).
